component_identification.content: Official components include 7 easels, 7 dry-erase markers, 7 cleaning cloths, and a deck of word cards (110 cards, 550 words in common editions), plus rules. During setup, each player takes one easel and marker; one player is active guesser each round while all others become clue writers. Word cards are drawn and oriented so only clue writers can see the target entry while guesser cannot. For café maintenance, ensure marker ink is legible and cloths are present; physical wear affects readability more than card wear in this title.

core_game_loop.content: On a round, one player is the guesser and cannot see the secret word. Every other player writes a one-word clue on their easel. Before showing clues to the guesser, all clue writers compare and erase duplicates so only unique clues remain. The guesser then sees the surviving clues and makes one guess at the target word. Resolve as success or failure, rotate guesser, and continue cooperatively through the agreed card count.

detailed_rules.content: Setup distributes writing materials and defines round count (often the official 13-card sequence for score grading). Each round, reveal a new card so non-guessers can view the selected target while guesser looks away. All clue writers independently write exactly one clue. Then comes the mandatory duplicate-elimination step: identical clues are removed before the guesser sees anything; many tables also treat close inflections/plurals as duplicates when clearly equivalent, but the strictest official baseline is exact duplicate words. After erasing duplicates, reveal remaining clues. Guesser gives one spoken answer. If correct, place card in success pile; if wrong, card is lost per scoring method. Rotate guesser role clockwise and repeat. Communication rules matter: no additional hints, gestures, or tone cues after clue reveal; the deduction must come from written clues only. If all clues are eliminated, the guesser must still attempt (or pass under allowed variant). Keep pace brisk: writing, compare, erase, reveal, guess, score. Additional rulebook-grounded teaching clarifications: (1) Always announce phase order before acting so the table knows when decisions lock in. (2) Resolve challenges, blocks, votes, and reveals in the printed order without skipping windows, since many disputes come from out-of-order shortcuts. (3) Keep hidden information boundaries strict: only information explicitly allowed by the rules may be shared. (4) Maintain public state carefully—discard areas, score tracks, captured tricks, or revealed tokens should stay visible to all players. (5) When a card or role creates an exception, that exception overrides the normal pattern only for its stated scope. (6) For café teaching, perform one narrated sample cycle using real components before the first competitive round. (7) If an edition difference exists, read the exact printed text in the box being played and apply that wording over memory or online summaries. (8) End-of-round housekeeping (draw up, rotate role, clear temporary markers, refresh protections) should be completed fully before starting the next round. (9) If a state is illegal due to a missed mandatory rule, rewind only to the last clean decision point and replay transparently. (10) Encourage players to restate objective conditions in plain language to reduce accidental losses caused by misunderstanding victory timing.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Just One is fully cooperative: team score equals number of correctly guessed words at session end. Standard official play uses a fixed number of rounds/cards (commonly 13) and then checks result against performance bands in the rulebook (from poor to excellent/legendary style ratings by edition language). Wrong guesses add to failure count and reduce final score ceiling. End game when planned card count is exhausted; no individual winner exists.

beginner_strategy.content: Write clues that are specific but not obvious duplicates—“mammal” and “animal” can coexist, while two players writing “pet” wastes both. Think one step beyond the first association you expect everyone else to choose. As guesser, infer meaning from the pattern of what survived; a weird clue that remains is often the key. In repeated sessions, vary clue style (category, function, context) so your group avoids habitual collisions. If teaching newcomers, run a sample round to demonstrate duplicate cancellation before score begins. Practical first-play checklist: confirm objective, identify your legal choices, and verify what information is private before committing each turn.
